coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-thionine modified aptamer-based biosensor for highly sensitive cocaine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Azizi, Sajad
Gholivand, Mohammad Bagher
Amiri, Masoud
Manouchehri, Iraj
Moradian, Rostam
coredata.dc:description: Due to the application of aptasensors in biosensing, researchers are encouraged to design and develop sensing platforms with greater sensitivity and selectivity. This report introduces an aptasensor whose capture DNA is immobilized on the surface of an ITO electrode modified with a gold nanoparticle (prepared with physical vapor deposition technique). Here, target DNA combined with carbon dots that are modified with thionine to provide an oxidation–reduction redox probe to monitor cocaine. In the presence of cocaine, the aptamer conformational change, was take placed and reduced the distance between thionine and the electrode surface, and increased the charge transfer efficiency. Under optimal conditions our cocaine biosensor shows a dynamic range of 10–70 pM with a detection limit of 0.26 pM. Determination of cocaine in human serum and urine samples shows the practical application of the proposed aptasensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensors
Carbon dots
Physical vapor deposition
Cocaine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the mixed solution containing target DNA and Cocaine.
The morphology and electrochemical characterization of ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM images of CDs and Th-CDs. (B) The DLS diagram of CDs and Th-CDs, (C) UV–Vis spectrums, (D) fluorescence (E) and FT-IR of Th, CDs, Th-CDs and Th-CDs- tDNA.

(A) CV of Th and Th-CDs were recorded with ITO/Au/MER electrodes in the 0.1 M PBS pH = 7.0. (B) The DPV of the ITO/Au /MER electrodes used for the experiments is shown in Fig. 2A; after washing and transfer to 0.1 M PBS pH = 7.0.
The AFM images of (A) ITO, (B) ITO/Au (before heating treatment), (C) ITO/Au (after heating treatment) and (D) ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA.

A) CVs and B) Impedance spectra (Nyquist plots) of bare ITO/Au (a), bare ITO (b) ITO/Au/MER (c), ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA (d) and cocaine-Th-CDs-tDNA-/ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA (e) recorded in the solution of 0.1 M KCl containing 5.0 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4.

(A) The effect of concentrations of Th-CDs-tDNA on the electrochemical signal of 30 pM cocaine at the ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode with incubation time of 25 min. (B) The effect of DNA hybridization time of 30 pM cocaine and 110 μg mL−1 Th-CDs-tDNA. The analytical signals were obtained by DPV technique in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4).

(A) DPV responses of the ssDNA/MER/ITO recorded in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and the different concentrations of cocaine. (0,10,20,30,40,50,60,70, pM) (B) Calibration curve of DPV peak current versus cocaine concentration.

A) DPV signal: the selectivity of this system against 70 pM cocaine, 100 nM morphine, 100 nM ATP, and 100 nM diazepam and100 nM caffeine and finally their mixture. B) The signal of 50 pM of cocaine for one electrode in 8 days was monitored to study the stability of sensor.
Compare the results of this work with other methods used to detect cocaine.

row.entry: Using aptamer on surface of modified gold nanoparticles
Based on silica nanoparticles modified with streptavidin, and works via double-quenching system of fluorophore and cocaine split aptamer
It works based on a composite of gold nanoparticle-modified molybdenum disulfide and the attachment of aptamer and cocaine on it.
Using nanoporous gold particles besides immobilization of modified labeled aptamer.
It works based on aggregation of gold nanoparticles, aptamer and a composite of molybdenum disulfide.
It based on a single nanochannel coupled with DNA aptamers.

entry.underline: Using capacitive monitoring of sensor surface

row.entry: It works on the basis of modifying a gold electrode with aptamer and blocking agents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The result of 3 measurement of a solutions of 50 pM of cocaine with three different sensors.
Compare results obtained by spiking in urine and serum.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-thionine modified aptamer-based biosensor for highly sensitive cocaine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah 671496734, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah 671496734, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Department of Physics, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, Department of Physics, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Research Center, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Research Center, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the application of aptasensors in biosensing, researchers are encouraged to design and develop sensing platforms with greater sensitivity and selectivity. This report introduces an aptasensor whose capture DNA is immobilized on the surface of an ITO electrode modified with a gold nanoparticle (prepared with physical vapor deposition technique). Here, target DNA combined with carbon dots that are modified with thionine to provide an oxidation–reduction redox probe to monitor cocaine. In the presence of cocaine, the aptamer conformational change, was take placed and reduced the distance between thionine and the electrode surface, and increased the charge transfer efficiency. Under optimal conditions our cocaine biosensor shows a dynamic range of 10–70 pM with a detection limit of 0.26 pM. Determination of cocaine in human serum and urine samples shows the practical application of the proposed aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical biosensors such as immune sensor or aptasensors [1–3] are the state-of-the-art devices, which provide simple, inexpensive and effective ways for determination of long lists of biological, organic or inorganic analytes. Since, the aptamers are made of nucleic acids prepared by SELEX method [4], they have more simple maintenance conditions and lower price. In addition, working with aptamer is significantly easier than with antibodies.

The recognition element, such as aptamers, enable biosensors to detect a wide range of analytes. Generally, in an electrochemical biosensor, in order to produce or obtain analytical signals, three parts in a typical sensor must work together. First, a conductive platform, which is not only a proper surface for electrochemical reaction, but also it provides a suitable surface for immobilization of recognition element. The second part is the recognition elements itself (such as an aptamers) that by means of their selective (or specific) interactions with analyte, enable the sensor to recognize a specific analyte [5]. Finally, when an analyte is recognized by recognition elements the related electrochemical signal (due to the interaction between analyte and recognition elements) must be recorded.

Generally, an electrochemical biosensor works based on monitoring a change in the value of electrical circuit elements such as voltage (E), current (I) or resistance (R) as analytical signal. In order to have/produce a powerful analytical signal and find its logical relationship with the concentration of an analyte, some strategies have been considered. First, is obtaining a conductive and sensitive surface as working electrode. This modified surface can be used for direct detection of an electroactive analyte or can be used for immobilization of aptamers (as recognition element) [2,3,6,7]. Another strategy is based on the application of the labeled biomolecules and components such as labeled aptamers [8]. These labeled components produce a selective analytical signal especially for non-electroactive analytes such as proteins.

In term of surface modification, different techniques and materials have been used to present a suitable electrochemical electrode. For example, in order to prepare a suitable electrode, different conductive surfaces (used as electrochemical sensors) have been modified with composites and materials like: conductive polymers [3,9], gold nanoparticles or graphene [10,11], poly (dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride)-decorated MXene [12], spindle-like α-Fe2O3 Mesocrystals composited with reduced graphene oxide [13] and etc. Meanwhile, physical vapor deposition (PVD) is a practical technique for covering and modification of large surfaces with thin layers of metals [2,14]. The PVD provides an inexpensive, simple method for designing very reproducible surfaces. In fact, since by means of application of PVD technique, synthesis of layers with nanometer thickness is applicable, so using precious metal such as Au is not expensive anymore. In addition, due to the physical nature of the synthesis of nanostructures and the absence of the use of chemicals, this method can be presented as a green method.

In terms of electrochemical signaling, using electroactive organic dyes such as methylene blue or neutral red, has given rise to synthesis of new electrochemical probes [2,5]. Unlike complex electrochemical probes such as enzymatic probes, which are made of proteins, organic dyes are small molecules which are inexpensive and efficient in different media and solutions. Moreover, these molecules have versatile abilities for interaction and conjugation with different nanomaterials, which giving rise to the designing and manufacturing of new brands of nanomaterials with much simple methods. One category of the proper nanomaterials, which has attracted a large amount of interest is carbon dot (CD) materials. The CDs are easy to synthesize, not toxic and have different suitable functional groups (for conjugation with other molecules and materials).

Cocaine, also known as benzoylmethylecgonine, is a strong stimulant used mostly as an illegal recreational drug. The drug abuse is a serious global public health problem and a contributing factor to severe social problems. Ultrasensitive and selective detection of this kind compounds at nano or sub-nanomolar level is very important for many areas such as early diagnosis and drug testing, healthcare, food, and environment. Conventional methods for cocaine detection are mostly mass spectrometry based analytical techniques or high pressure liquid chromatography assays coupled with various detection methods such as UVabsorbance or fluorescence. These methods have limits of detection (LOD) around nanomolar (nM) level, and they are often associate with shortcomings such as being time consuming, high cost, requires skilled personnel and complex sample preparation. Hence,there is an unmet need for the development of an easy and low cost method for cocaine detection in complex biological fluids. In the report of Rebecca A. Jufer [15], at a cutoff concentration of 300 ng m L-1, urine cocaine detection times ranged from 10 to 22 days. Cone and Weddington [16], also reported an extended elimination phase for cocaine in saliva and urine. They monitored saliva and urine samples collected periodically during cocaine withdrawal. Cocaine was detected in saliva for up to 10 days and in urine for up to 15 days with radioimmunoassay (sensitivity = 0.5 ng mL−1). The mean saliva cocaine elimination half-life was estimated as 68.2 h. There are a lot of reports about determination of cocaine in plasma and or whole blood. In one report [17] an anticocaine aptamer-based sorbent has been used for the selective extraction of cocaine from human plasma and then its determination. In the other report [18] a combinatorial platform by using quantum dots (QDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as well as a functional aptamer has been constructed which selectively recognizes cocaine and its metabolite benzoylecgonine (BE) etc.

Here, we report on a new electrochemical sensor for determination cocaine as a well-known addictive stimulant drug [19,20]. In this regard, an ITO electrode was covered by (Au) nanostructure, using PVD technique following by heat-treatment. Then, the as-prepared surface was modified with a layer of thiol -functionalized end of an aptamer sequence. In the second step the synthesized CD, which was modified with thionine molecule (as an electroactive organic molecule) was covalently bonded to the other aptamer sequence. Finally, through a combination of modified electrode and probe we presented a cocaine biosensor, which is sensitive to ultra-low concentrations of cocaine with linear range of 10–70 pM and 0.26 pM as the limit of detection. Moreover, the electrode is applied for determination of cocaine in biological samples.

The 2-mercaptoethanol and 6-mercaptohexanoic acid, thiamine, 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), N-(3-Di-methylaminopropyl)- N′ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and methyl thiazolyldiphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1- Piperazine ethane sulfonic acid) HEPES buffer, K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6] were obtained from Merck (Darmst Germany). Cocaine was obtained from Drug Control Headquarters (Tehran, Iran) and DNA aptamer [21] was from Aminsan Co. (Tehran, Iran):
cDNA: 5ʹ-GGGAGTCAAGAACGAAAAA NH2 C6-3ʹ
tDNA: 5ʹ P-AAATTCGTTCTTCAATGAAGTGGGACGACA-3ʹ

An Autolab potentiostate-galvanostat device (PGSTAT 302 N, the Netherlands) equipped with a NOVA (2.1). A typical cell contains three electrodes, working (modified ITO sheet with surface area = 3.14 mm2), reference (saturated Ag/AgCl) and an auxiliary (Pt wire). The morphology of ITO electrode during modifications were monitored by different spectroscopic techniques such as atomic force microscope (AFM, Nanosurf model mobile S), Agilent 8453 diode array spectrophotometer, USA), and a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer equipped with a quartz microcell (Agilent, USA). The Philips transmission electron microscope (TEM) device used for study the morphology of carbon quantum dots.

The solvothermal method [2] was applied for synthesis of CDs. Briefly, glucose solution (100 mL solution of 0.4 g mL−1) was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 180C for ° 12 h. Then, centrifugation of the resulted CDs at 10,000 rpm was carried out to separate and the supernatant from the bigger particles. The CDs showed a yellowish luminescence suggesting successful synthesis of CD.

The activation of carboxyl groups of CDs was done by stirring the PBS solution (20 mL of 1 mM, pH 6) containing NHS (15 mM), EDC.HCl (30 mM) and 50 mL of CDs solution for 30 min. To this solution 5 mg of thionine was added and stirred for 40 min. Finally, for separation of the resulted Th-Cds, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm and washed three times to remove the nonreacted CDs and Th. Th-CDs were dried under reduced pressure and stored before use [22].

The tDNA (5ʹ phosphorylated) (0.1 mmol) was activated in a solution of EDC (10 mM) and 1-methyl imidazole (20 mM, pH 6) at 37 °C with constant shaking for 1.5 h. Then, 10 mL of the activated tDNA was added to 10 mL of CDs-Th (0.1 mg mL−1 in HEPES buffer (0.2 M, pH 8)) and stirred for 18 h at 55 °C. The product (Th-CD-tDNA) was centrifuged (10000 rpm) to remove the excess of EDC, 1-methyl imidazole [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the mixed solution containing target DNA and Cocaine.
ce:section.ce:para: A stock of cocaine (0.1 mM) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4) containing (100 μg mL−1 in PBS) a stock of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) containing Th-CD-tDNA (100 μg mL−1 in PBS) were used for preparation of different concentrations of cocaine (10–70 pM) solutions.

A layer of Au was deposited on a flexible ITO substrate via thermal evaporation in vacuum (pressure: 9 × 10-6 mb). Highly pure Au (99.99 %) granules were put into a tungsten boat and melted to evaporate and deposited on the substrates. The deposition time lasted for 30 s. Finally, the ITO-Au sheet was put in the oven at 120 °C for 10 h.

ITO/Au electrode was immersed in a 25 mL ethanol containing 1.0 mM of 6-mercaptohexanoic acid (MER has a carboxylic groups) and 0.2 mM 2-mercaptoethanol, (molar ratio 4/1) for 40 min. Then, the electrode (ITO/Au/MER) is washed with ethanol and water and transferred to a buffer of 0.05 MES with pH 6 containing 10 mL EDC/NHS (2 mM/1 mM) for 30 min for activation of carboxylic groups. Then the electrode removed from the solution and washed and dried and finally, 5 μL of 50 μM ssDNA (cDNA) solution was drop on its surface. The incubation was continued for 2 h at ambient condition (during this step, the electrode was placed in a sealed container to avoid the evaporation the solution). Ultimately, the as prepared electrode, ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA, was stored at 4 °C. The steps of fabrication of the aptasensor have been depicted in Scheme1 .

As shown in scheme 1, the Th-CD was functionalized with the tDNA. The thiolated nucleic acid (cDNA) was assembled on an Au electrode. In the presence of cocaine, the supramolecular complex consisting of cocaine and two aptamer subunits is formed on the electrode and created electrochemical signal. The same results were reported previously [21,24] in the absence of cocaine we observed no electrochemical signal. Here, the ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode was put in solutions containing different concentrations of cocaine (10–70 pM) and Th-CDs-tDNA (100 μg mL−1). After 50 min incubation the electrode was removed from each solution and rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4) and transferred to a voltammetric cell containing 10 mL PBS (pH 7.4) for electrochemical measurements. The concentration of cocaine was determined by DPV technique with “signal on” attitude. In the absence of cocaine, no signal was observed but in the presence of cocaine the related analytical signal was observed.

The analysis of human serum and urine spiked with different concentrations of cocaine as the real samples were performed by a standard addition method (n = 3). Here, serum and urine samples were obtained from healthy volunteers. The serum samples were then separated from the red blood cells by centrifugation (4000 rpm) and stored at 4° C for use. Urine was also centrifuged and then 2 mL of each sample was transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask and spiked with different concentration of cocaine and then it was diluted to 10 mL with 0.1 M phosphate buffer.

Characterization of CDs, Th-CDs and Th-CDs - tDNA were carried out by different spectroscopic techniques. As the Scheme 1 shows, the Th-CDs-tDNA was prepared in three steps. First the CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method and after the attachment of Th to CDs, it was used for preparation of the electrochemical probe (Th-CDs-tDNA). Fig. 1 A is the TEM image of the Th-CDs. The image shows that the particles have diameters about 40–65 nm. It seems the shape of the Th-CDs is little spherical. However, in order to take the TEM image, the sample is dried in vacuum, hence, they can aggregate and form these structures. Hence, for study of the size of particles in solutions we conducted the DLS experiment. As the Fig. 1B illustrates, the CDs particles have hydrodynamic radius of about 135 nm but after synthesis of Th-CDs the radius increase to 170 nm. These studies show after formation of Th-CDs, the hydrodynamic radius increased about 50 nm therefore, most of the Th-CDs particles do not aggregate to gather (otherwise the minimum hydrodynamic radius must be more than 270 nm). Fig. 1C shows the UV–Vis spectrums of Th, CDs, Th-CDs and Th-CDs-tDNA. At the visible region the CDs doesn’t show any absorption peak but the pure Th has a peak at about 600 nm and a shoulder at about 560 nm that are attributed of monomeric form of Th and its H-type dimer aggregate respectively [25]. When the Th was bonded to CDs not only the UV absorption bands of CDs (230 nm) and Th (284 nm) increased but also enhanced the monomeric peak of Th (600 nm), which shows the attachment of Th to CDs. The Th-CDs-DNA almost shows a same peak, with some red shifts, at 287 and 603 nm.

CDs are known for their unique fluorescence activity [26]. Hence, Th-CD-tDNA was studied by fluorescence. Fig. 1D shows the fluorescence spectra of Th, CD, Th-CD and Th-CDs-tDNA. CDs (λex = 370) have a wild peak at 530 nm and Th shows another wide peak at 497 nm with a weak shoulder of about 420 nm. After synthesis of Th-CDs, as the Fig. 1D shows that the Th-CD spectrum not only has a wider peak of about 501 nm that covers the Th and CD peaks, but also a small peak (like a weak shoulder related to Th) at 414 nm. Finally, the Th-CDs-tDNA, which was synthesized via conjugation of tDNA to Th-CDs, shows two peaks at 424 nm and 475 nm. The changes of the behavior of the fluorescence spectrum of each material prove the formation of the final product.

Since the FT-IR is a powerful technique for studying the functional groups of different materials, we also studied the Th, CDs, Th-CDs and Th-CDs-tDNA spectra (with this technique). In term of CDs, as it was expected, the FT-IR spectrum showed characteristic peaks of different carbon and oxygen based functional groups (Fig. 1E). For example, the FT-IR of CDs shows some peaks at 3413 cm−1 (Hydroxyl group; H-bonded, (OH stretch), 3470 cm−1 (Dimeric OH stretch) 3556 (Internally bonded OH stretch), 2935 cm−1 (CH asym./sym. Stretch) 2880 cm−1 (CH stretch) 1712 cm−1 (CO, carboxylic and carbonyl 1621 (CC aromatic) etc. The Th also has its own characteristic peaks. Here, the Th spectrum shows some peaks at 3320 cm−1 and 3131 cm−1 (NH stretching [27]) 1620 cm−1 (NH bending) 1322 cm−1 (CN aromatic amine stretching) 1498 cm−1 (Th skeletal vibrations related to the aromatic rings). After preparation of Th-CDs via interaction of amine groups (from Th) and carboxylic acid groups (from CDs) the resulted FT-IR spectrum shows new characters. As the Fig. 1D illustrates, a strong band at 1569 cm−1 (CO stretching from secondary amide [28]), 1390 cm−1 (CC stretching from secondary amide [28]). a wide band at 3379 cm−1 (OH; stretch, or NH aromatic primary amine; stretch) and 1215 cm − 1 (CN stretching from secondary amide [28]). After conjugation of Th-CDs to tDNA and formation of Th-CDs-tDNA the related FT-IR spectrum shows some characteristic bands related to aptamer. In fact, the FT-IR spectrum has some bands at 1622 cm−1 (NC and CC ring vibration of bases or NH bending vibrations of the base residues [29,30]) 1029 cm−1 (PO2 -1, stretching and CO stretching related to the ribose and deoxyribose) proving the formation of Th-CDs-tDNA.

In order to use a molecule as an electrochemical probe it must show a strong and repeatable signal. Thionine as an organic molecule with well-known two electrons two protons/electrons reaction, is suitable for electrochemical biosensing applications [3,31].

In order to show the electrochemical ability of Th-CDs as a suitable electrochemical probe we used the CV and DPV techniques. As Fig. 2 A illustrates, the CVs of Th (0.05 mM, with ΔE = 0.10 V with ITO/Au/MER electrode) and Th-CDs (0.1 mg mL−1, with ΔE = 0.13 V with ITO/Au/MER electrode) both have a redox electrochemical behavior with strong signal (current). However, Th faces a major problem that prevents it from being used as an electrochemical probe. As mentioned before, an electrochemical probe must show a repeatable analytical signal. Electrodes immersed in Th solution show a strong memory effect. To show this effect, we examined the DVP signal of the electrode. Here, two ITO/Au /MER electrodes used for the CV test in Fig. 2A, were washed and transferred to a buffer solution and their DPV signal were recorded. The electrode that was used in Th solution, has a strong memory effect (Fig. 2B). However, the electrode which was used for recording the CV of Th-CDs-tDNA (illustrated in Fig. 2B) does not show a significant memory effect. As such, we used Th-CDs as an ideal electrochemical probe for designing our electrochemical biosensor. This observation may be due to the fact that Th, unlike Th-CDs-tDNA, has free amine groups that can interact with the electrode surface. Hence, this significant memory effect can be attributed to this nature of free Th.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and electrochemical characterization of ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA
ce:section.ce:para: The steps of manufacturing of ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode were monitored with AFM technique. As Fig. 3 A depicts, the bare ITO does not have very smooth surface and cross-sectional data shows a typical roughness [2] in the range of ± 7 nm. When a layer of Au is deposited on the surface of ITO (Fig. 3B), the AFM image shows different morphology with smoother surface and cross-sectional analysis proves the roughness is in the range of ± 4 nm. In the next step, where the ITO is treated in 120 °C (Fig. 3C), some nano-structures are observable on the surface of heat-treated ITO/Au electrode. Moreover, the cross-sectional study shows these nanostructures with height of 6 nm while the interval between two nanostructures has smoother surface roughness. Finally, ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode (Fig. 3D) has its special morphology that is different from that of ITO/Au which shows the attachment of the aptamer sequence. The cross-sectional analysis also indicates the enhancement in the surface roughness to ± 40 nm proving the formation of ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode.

The electrochemical behavior of the fabricated electrodes during modification in 0.1 M KCl containing 5.0 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-) was studied by CV and EIS techniques. As the CV studies shows, the bare ITO electrode, reveals a redox CV curve with a peak potential separation (ΔEp) of 210 mV. As the ITO electrode was modified with Au layer, the related current of the redox probe was significantly increased with smaller ΔEp. This observation can be attributed to the higher conductivity and higher surface area of ITO/Au electrode. In order to prepare the ITO/Au for immobilization of aptamer, the surface of ITO/Au was modified with a layer of MER molecules. As Fig. 4A indicates, the related CV of ITO/Au/MER electrode was accompanied by smaller current signal and wider ΔEp (=220 mV) proving the successful formation of self-assembled monolayer of MER molecules. After the immobilization of apatmer sequence (curve d), the further decreases was observed in peak current and reversibility (ΔEp = 290 mV) of the resulted electrode (ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode) indicating the apatamer molecules with negative charges have successfully immobilized on the surface of the electrode. Finally, when the ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode was treated in solution of Th-CDs-tDNA and analyte, the related CV showed more decrease in the peak currents and the ΔEp increased to 30 mV. In fact, when the Th-CDs-tDNA and cocaine interact with ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA and formation the final structure of apatmer-target on surface of electrode, the accessibility for redox probe is got more limited and the redox current was decreased. The EIS studies (Fig. 4B) also show the same CV trend. Each EIS spectrum has a semicircle, at high frequencies, related to faradaic reaction and a slant line related to Warburg resistance at lower frequencies. As the ITO electrode is modified with Au, the Rct is decreased and then in the other steps of modification, the Rct value was increased.

In order to have the best analytical signal, the parameters affecting the electrochemical signal of biosensor were optimized. Here, as the scheme 1 illustrates the formation of target-aptamer complex occurs in the presence of Th-CDs-tDNA and cocaine on surface of modified electrode ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA. Hence, we optimized concentration of Th-CDs-tDNA and the incubation time of Th-CDs-DNA and cocaine. In order to optimum concentration of Th-CDs-tDNA, the ITO/Au/MER/ssDNA electrode was immersed in a solution containing different concentration of Th-CDs-tDNA and 30 pM of cocaine and after 25 min, the electrode in each case was removed and transferred into to buffer solution (PBS pH = 7.4), then its electrochemical signal was recorded by DPV technique. As Fig. 5 A indicates, the DPV current leveled off at the concentrations higher than 110 μg mL−1 of Th-CDs-tDNA. In order to optimum the incubation time of Th-CDs-DNA and cocaine, the similar experiment was carried out in solution of cocaine (30 pM) and Th-CDs-tDNA (110 μg mL−1) with incubation times ranging from 5 to 35 min. As it is clear from Fig. 5B, the best results were observed at incubation time of 25 min. Hence, the concentration of 110 μg mL−1 of Th-CDs-tDNA with 25 min incubation time were chosen as the optimum conditions.

At the end, each sensor must be used for determination of its target. As the Table 3 shows, the recoveries values are in the range of 98 to 106 % with RSD % less than 1.77 %. As the results illustrate, the biosensor has high ability for determination of cocaine in real samples with complex matrix (human serum and urine), which is an important factor for a biosensor.

In conclusion, the present study has demonstrated the monitoring of cocaine by new biosensor based on formation of supramolecular complexes composed of aptamer subunits and cocaine on transducer surfaces. The biosensor showed a good selectivity and excellent sensitivity toward cocaine over other compounds as interfering substances. This new biosensor shows better cocaine sensitivity and selectivity over traditional sole aptamer-based cocaine sensors, which confirms that the formation of supramolecular complexes with functional aptamers is expected to be an effective way to design new biosensing devices for ultrasensitive and selective drug detections in the fields of medicine, healthcare, food, and environment.

Sajad Azizi: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Bagher Gholivand: Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Masoud Amiri: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Iraj Manouchehri: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Rostam Moradian: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzyme-free sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for highly sensitive prostate specific antigen based on conjugation of quantum dots and antibody on surface of modified glassy carbon electrode with core-shell magnetic metal-organic frameworks
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ehzari, M. Amiri, M. Safari, Enzyme-free sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for highly sensitive prostate specific antigen based on conjugation of quantum dots and antibody on surface of modified glassy carbon electrode with core-shell magnetic metal-organic frameworks, Talanta. 210 (2020) 120641. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA biosensor based on surface modification of ITO by physical vapor deposition of gold and carbon quantum dots modified with neutral red as an electrochemical redox probe
Microchem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Azizi, M.-B. Gholivand, M. Amiri, I. Manouchehri, DNA biosensor based on surface modification of ITO by physical vapor deposition of gold and carbon quantum dots modified with neutral red as an electrochemical redox probe, Microchem. 159 (2020) 105523. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105523.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Prostate Specific Antigen Immunosensor Based on Biotinylated-Antibody/Cyclodextrin Inclusion Complex: Fabrication and Electrochemical Studies
Electroanalysis
